quiz_info.sottotitolo: 10 real scenarios. Honest answers. Discover your blind spots.
quiz_info.istruzioni: Every scenario is something that's already happened to you or will. Don't pick the 'right' answer - pick what you'd actually do at 11:47 PM, tired, thumb on your phone. Honesty matters more than performance here.

punteggio.0-8: Bulletproof - Your BS detector works just fine
punteggio.9-16: Healthy sceptic - Good instincts, a few blind spots
punteggio.17-24: Exposed - More vulnerable than you think
punteggio.25-40: Fertile ground - Manipulation techniques find you receptive

narrative.intro_message:
Every day you scroll through hundreds of pieces of content. Some are information, others are designed to fool you - and the difference isn't always obvious.

This test puts you in front of 10 real situations: viral posts, TV debates, group messages, YouTube videos. Stuff you encounter every week.

There's no grade and it's not an exam. It's a mirror: it shows you where your defences hold and where they have holes. Some answers will surprise you.
narrative.difficulty_curve: Scenarios get progressively more subtle. The first ones test instinct, the last ones test reasoning.

badges.name: Clean start
badges.description: You spotted the trap on the first try

badges.name: On a roll
badges.description: Three in a row without hesitation

badges.name: Five for five
badges.description: Five consecutive - your critical thinking is in shape

badges.name: Sharp instinct
badges.condition: Complete the quiz in under 3 minutes

badges.name: Wants to understand
badges.description: The answer isn't enough - you want to know why

badges.name: Clear-headed
badges.condition: Answer q4 and q8 correctly (emotional traps)
badges.description: Emotions don't make you lose your head

badges.name: Logic antibodies
badges.condition: Answer q7 and q10 correctly (logical traps)
badges.description: You see beyond patterns - your brain doesn't fall for bias

streak_messages.3: 🔥 Three in a row - 'On a roll' badge unlocked.
streak_messages.5: ⚡ Five consecutive. Your critical thinking is in shape.
streak_messages.7: Seven out of seven. Sure you're not cheating?
streak_messages.10: Ten out of ten. You don't have a single blind spot.

questions.title: The viral post
questions.visual_cue: [Social post with 15K shares, blurry image of a tap with a red halo, CAPS LOCK headline, pixelated site logo, enthusiastic comments from stock photo profiles]
questions.situation: You're scrolling at 11:47 PM. A post with 15,000 shares: "Tap water contains CHROMIUM DISSITATE-7! They've been HIDING it for YEARS!" The site is "hiddentruth247.xyz", the author signs as 'Dr. Independent Researcher'. In the comments, stock photo profiles write "I KNEW IT!!!".
questions.question: Your thumb is hovering over 'Share'. What do you do?

options.text: Stop thumb. Open Google and search 'chromium dissitate-7' + fact-check
options.explanation: Top Google result: 'Chromium dissitate-7 does not exist on the periodic table.' They tried with a chemical-sounding name - and you didn't bite. Your brain is worth more than 15,000 thumbs.

options.text: 15,000 people can't ALL be wrong - there must be something to it
options.explanation: You just activated the 'social proof' bias. Know how many people have shared that the Earth is flat? Millions. Virality measures emotion, not truth. 'Dissitate' doesn't exist - literally.

options.text: I share to warn my family - better safe than sorry!
options.explanation: With the best intentions, you just became a disinformation vector. 68% of health hoaxes spread this way: good people who don't verify. The manipulator thanks you.

options.text: I comment 'Source?' under the post and wait for a reply
options.explanation: Asking for a source is better than sharing, but waiting for 'hiddentruth247.xyz' to provide evidence is like asking a cat to fetch. You verify actively.

questions.bonus_insight: Did you know? .xyz, .tk and .ml domains cost less than a coffee and require no verification. 47% of disinformation sites use these domains. If the URL looks like your neighbour's Wi-Fi password, it's probably not Reuters.

questions.title: The election circus
questions.visual_cue: [TV studio: two politicians shouting simultaneously, the moderator checking their phone, the banner reads 'EXCLUSIVE - TOTAL CLASH', volume at maximum]
questions.situation: Prime-time election debate. Candidate A: 'My opponent ran THREE companies into the ground!' Candidate B: 'Oh yeah? YOU evaded TAXES!' The moderator is tweeting. Neither cites a single source. The studio audience applauds like it's a rock concert.
questions.question: You're forming an opinion. But based on what, exactly?

options.text: Based on nothing: these are unsupported accusations. This is wrestling, not politics
options.explanation: You just unmasked 'political theatre': maximum noise, zero substance. A real debate has data, sources, concrete proposals. This one had only decibels.

options.text: If one were lying, the other would nail them with evidence, right?
options.explanation: You're assuming politicians on TV play fair. In a ring with no referee, both can bluff. The absence of a data-backed rebuttal is never proof of truth.

options.text: Tomorrow I'll check both claims through independent sources
options.explanation: You don't settle for the show and go check afterwards. You're the kind of viewer that gives spin doctors nightmares.

options.text: The one who shouts louder is probably wrong
options.explanation: That's also a bias: judging by tone rather than content. Sometimes the loud one is right, sometimes the quiet one is lying. Volume isn't an indicator.

questions.bonus_insight: Did you know? Reuters Institute studies show that 73% of claims in election debates contain verifiable distortions. Nearly 3 out of 4 statements aren't fully true. Imagine a weather forecast that's accurate 27% of the time.

questions.title: Same event, two realities
questions.visual_cue: [Two front pages side by side: left has 'HISTORIC PROTEST: 500,000 IN THE STREETS!' with aerial crowd photo; right has 'TOTAL FLOP: a few thousand wandering around' with near-empty square and pigeons]
questions.situation:
Same day. Same climate protest. Two different outlets.

Outlet A: 'HISTORIC PROTEST: 500,000 IN THE STREETS!' - aerial photo of a packed square.
Outlet B: 'TOTAL FLOP: a few thousand wandering around' - photo of an empty corner with pigeons.

Both swear they're telling the truth. Both have 'photographic evidence'.
questions.question: Two parallel realities. How do you navigate?

options.text: I read both and check police or official third-party estimates
options.explanation: You went for the primary source instead of picking your favourite narrative. Official counts (police, city officials) are imperfect but the most solid starting point.

options.text: One of them is clearly lying - I just need to figure out which
options.explanation: Often neither is 'lying' strictly speaking. 8AM photo vs 2PM photo, side street vs main square, partial count vs optimistic estimate. Framing isn't lying - it's selection. And it's even more insidious.

options.text: I trust the outlet I usually follow - they're always right
options.explanation: You're choosing the reality that feels like home. This is literally how information bubbles work: you end up in a parallel universe where you're 'always right'. Spoiler: nobody is always right.

options.text: I search for amateur videos and geotagged photos on social media
options.explanation: User-generated content with geotags and timestamps is great for triangulation. But watch out: even videos can be decontextualised (old footage passed off as current).

questions.bonus_insight: Did you know? Photos lie more than words. An empty square at 8AM doesn't disprove a full square at 2PM. Angle changes everything: wide-angle from below = ocean of people, telephoto from above = 'four people and a dog'. Same square, same time.

questions.title: The emotional bait
questions.visual_cue: [Article with red headline: 'THE RICH ARE STEALING WATER FROM THE POOR - SCANDAL!!!', stock photo of a sad child, flashing 'SHARE FOR JUSTICE' button, zero graphs in the text]
questions.situation:
Heart-attack headline: 'THE RICH ARE STEALING WATER FROM THE POOR! SCANDAL!!!' The article has: a sad child photo (stock image, you recognise it), three tearful testimonials, zero numbers, zero data, zero sources, and a finale: 'If you have a heart, SHARE NOW!'

You feel the anger rising. Your fingers are tingling.
questions.question: Your emotions are maxed out. The 'Share' button is glowing. What do you do?

options.text: Strong emotion + zero data = red alert. I stop, breathe, and verify tomorrow with a clear head
options.explanation: You recognised that anger is the PRODUCT, not the side effect. Manipulators design content to make you feel first and think later (or never). You reversed the order.

options.text: I notice the lack of concrete data and the suspiciously aggressive call-to-action
options.explanation: You spotted the 'manipulation triangle': emotion + no evidence + share pressure. Serious content doesn't scream 'SHARE NOW'. At most it says 'source: ONS, page 47'.

options.text: If it's true it's outrageous - I share now, I'll verify later
options.explanation: You just did exactly what the manipulator wanted. The sequence 'share now, verify later' is like shooting first and asking questions after. Spoiler: you'll never verify. Nobody does.

options.text: I search for the same topic on major outlets to see if it's covered
options.explanation: If a real scandal of this magnitude existed, it would be on every outlet, not just 'justiceforall.blog'. The total absence of coverage is already a massive clue.

questions.bonus_insight: Did you know? Manipulators use 'emotional bait-and-switch': they anger you with the headline, the article proves nothing, but you've already shared before reading. 59% of people share articles after reading only the headline.

questions.title: The white coat
questions.visual_cue: [YouTube thumbnail: man in white coat in a fake lab (IKEA shelves in the background), clickbait title 'THE CURE THEY'RE HIDING FROM YOU!!!', 2M subscribers, 847K views]
questions.situation: Viral video: a man in a white coat, lab in the background (those shelves are definitely IKEA KALLAX, but whatever), explains authoritatively that 'mainstream medicine has been hiding the real cancer cure for 40 years'. The channel has 2 million subscribers. The video never shows: name, surname, degree, specialisation, hospital, publications.
questions.question: Two million people trust him. Do you?

options.text: 2 million subscribers = someone must have checked the credentials
options.explanation: Followers can be bought (100,000 for a few hundred quid). The algorithm rewards what keeps you glued, not what's true. The Flat Earth Society has millions of followers.

options.text: White coat doesn't mean doctor. No name, no credentials = no trust
options.explanation: You saw through the costume. A white coat costs £12 on Amazon. A real oncologist puts their name, hospital affiliation, and cites peer-reviewed studies. This guy has IKEA shelves.

options.text: I read the comments to see if anyone has debunked it
options.explanation: Critical comments get deleted, bots write fake praise, trolls attack doubters. Looking for truth in YouTube comments is like looking for a salad at a chip shop.

options.text: I search their name on PubMed or Google Scholar for published research
options.explanation: Searching for peer-reviewed publications is the pro move. Problem: you won't find their name because... they never gave it. And that tells you everything.

questions.bonus_insight: Did you know? The 'hidden cure' conspiracy has existed since the 1800s for every disease. If a secret cure really existed, at least one of the 15 million researchers worldwide would have revealed it. For the Nobel, for the fame, for the money, or for basic human decency.

questions.title: The personal attack
questions.visual_cue: [Talk show: Politician A showing charts about tax reform. Politician B ignores everything and holds up an old mugshot. The audience boos. On-screen text: 'CONVICTED 15 YEARS AGO']
questions.situation: Politician A presents a detailed tax reform: charts, projections, official data, simulations. Six months of work with economists. Politician B responds: 'All very nice, BUT this gentleman was convicted of fraud 15 years ago. Do you REALLY want to trust a CRIMINAL?' The audience murmurs. The charts are forgotten.
questions.question: Is the reform good or bad?

options.text: Classic ad hominem: the reform should be judged on its content, not on who proposes it
options.explanation: You identified the 'ad hominem' fallacy. Einstein was a terrible husband. Relativity still works. The charts need reading, not dismissing because the presenter has a questionable past.

options.text: The past conviction makes me doubt their honesty today too
options.explanation: Politician B just killed six months of technical work with one sentence. Didn't analyse a single number, didn't challenge a single data point. Just said 'bad person = bad proposal'. And you fell for it.

options.text: Both have a point: the reform should be analysed but the past matters too
options.explanation: A person's past can be broadly relevant, but here it was used specifically to avoid discussing the numbers. Go back to the charts: those are what matter.

options.text: If the reform is well-written, it doesn't matter who proposes it
options.explanation: Good policy remains good regardless of who proposes it. Even a broken clock is right twice a day. Judging ideas by their ideas is pure critical thinking.

questions.bonus_insight: Did you know? Ad hominem is politics' favourite fallacy because it works: it's easier to remember 'the convicted guy' than to understand a tax chart. The brain processes emotions 250ms before logic. 250 milliseconds that change elections.

questions.title: The avalanche of 'proof'
questions.visual_cue: [YouTube thumbnail of a 90-minute video: '100 PROOFS the GOVERNMENT is LYING!!!' with fiery red background, collage of politicians, 2.3M views, dramatic music]
questions.situation: 90-minute video: '100 IRREFUTABLE PROOFS the GOVERNMENT is LYING!' Each 'proof' lasts 50 seconds. No sources cited. But the editing is hypnotic: epic music, dramatic zooms, movie narration. After 30 minutes, you feel like 'it all fits'. The pieces slot together perfectly. Too perfectly.
questions.question: 90 minutes, 100 'proofs', zero sources. How do you react?

options.text: With 100 claims, at least some must be true
options.explanation: You just met the Gish Gallop. A technique invented by creationist Duane Gish: fire 100 claims so it's impossible to check them all. Debunk 10, 90 remain. Debunk 50, 'but what about the other 50?' A game you can't win playing by their rules.

options.text: I pick 5 at random and fact-check them. If 2-3 are false, the rest is suspect
options.explanation: Random sampling is exactly what real fact-checkers do. If 40% of the sample is false, the content is compromised. You don't need to debunk all 100. Smart.

options.text: I watch it all to form a complete and informed opinion
options.explanation: After 90 minutes of sourceless bombardment, your brain enters 'passive acceptance mode'. It's a documented neurological effect: repetition creates familiarity, familiarity creates trust. The manipulator doesn't want to convince you - they want to exhaust your critical thinking.

questions.bonus_insight: Did you know? Debunking a single lie takes 10 times longer than telling it. 100 lies at 50 seconds = 8.3 minutes. Debunking them all? About 83 minutes. It's the mathematics of manipulation.

questions.title: Auntie Karen's message
questions.visual_cue: [WhatsApp screenshot: message forwarded 47 times, all in CAPS LOCK, alarm emojis, 'YOU WON'T FIND THIS IN THE MEDIA!!!']
questions.situation:
WhatsApp, 'Extended Family' group (47 unread messages as usual). Auntie Karen has forwarded: 'SHARE NOW before they CENSOR this!!! The government is about to pass a law TONIGHT banning growing tomatoes on your balcony!!! You WON'T find this in the MEDIA because it's ALL being COVERED UP!!!'

The message has been forwarded 47 times.
questions.question: Auntie Karen is waiting for you to forward it. What do you do?

options.text: If it were true, it would be on every news site, TV channel and paper by tomorrow morning
options.explanation: No government in the world can pass laws secretly overnight without a single journalist noticing. The official gazette is public. If it's 'not in the media', maybe it's because it isn't true.

options.text: Better safe than sorry - I forward it now, I can always correct it later
options.explanation: 'Better safe than sorry' is the phrase behind 79% of WhatsApp chains. Nobody ever comes back to correct it. The message will live forever in that group, and in 6 months someone will forward it again as 'confirmation'.

options.text: I search 'balcony tomato ban law' on official sites. Reply to the group with the result
options.explanation: You save instead of forwarding, verify on official sources, then reply to the group with the results. The third step - educating Auntie Karen - is the bravest of all.

options.text: I ignore it - Auntie Karen sends these things every week
options.explanation: Ignoring is better than forwarding, but the message keeps circulating through the other 47 forwards. If you've got 30 seconds, a quick check and reply can break the chain.

questions.bonus_insight: Did you know? Artificial urgency is red flag number one. Rule of thumb: true information can wait for verification; false information needs your haste. If a message tells you 'SHARE NOW', it almost certainly wouldn't survive 5 minutes of Googling.

questions.title: The anonymous turncoat
questions.visual_cue: [Interview in silhouette: person's outline with distorted voice, yellow subtitles, title 'EX PHARMA EXEC REVEALS ALL', generic molecules in the background]
questions.situation: Viral video (3.7 million views): silhouette against a backlight, distorted voice. The interviewer introduces them as 'a former senior executive at a multinational pharmaceutical company - a courageous whistleblower risking their life for the truth'. For 22 minutes, they reveal disturbing secrets. But: no company name, no dates, no specific role, no documents shown.
questions.question: Mysterious silhouette + explosive secrets + zero verification. Credibility?

options.text: An 'ex' who speaks out has more credibility - they risk retaliation
options.explanation: Without a name, company, dates or role, anyone can sit in silhouette and say 'I was an executive'. Know what you need to make this video? A chair, a lamp behind you, and a script. Budget: zero.

options.text: I want verifiable details: which company, which role, which years, which documents
options.explanation: A real whistleblower (Snowden, Haugen, Assange) provides verifiable documents, accepts legal consequences, and gets identified. Total anonymity with no evidence is a red flag.

options.text: I share as a 'possible truth to keep an eye on'
options.explanation: Sharing unverified content as 'worth watching' is disinformation's Trojan horse. You create noise and give undeserved credibility. Doubt is resolved by verification, not by sharing.

options.text: If it were true, an investigative journalist would have already published with evidence
options.explanation: Investigative journalists live for stories like these. If 'the establishment' has been hiding something for years, one of thousands of journalists would have cracked it and won a Pulitzer.

questions.bonus_insight: Did you know? Quick comparison: Edward Snowden provided verified NSA documents and accepted real legal consequences. Frances Haugen testified before Congress with Facebook documents. The 'ex executive' in silhouette provided nothing verifiable.

questions.title: The perfect puzzle
questions.visual_cue: [Documentary: red lines connecting photos, dates, names on a corkboard. Crescendo music. 'COINCIDENCE? I THINK NOT.' Arrows everywhere, red circles]
questions.situation: YouTube documentary (4.8 million views, cinematic production). The narrator connects on a corkboard: a suspicious death of a researcher in 2019, a secret meeting between CEOs in 2020, a patent filed the day before the pandemic, an 'anomalous' stock purchase. Epic crescendo music. 'TOO MANY COINCIDENCES TO BE RANDOM!'
questions.question: Does the corkboard with the red lines convince you?

options.text: I verify each individual event independently before accepting any connection
options.explanation: Each puzzle piece must be verified on its own. Often just one turns out to be false or imprecise and the entire construction collapses. Did the researcher die of natural causes? Was the meeting public? Was the patent filed months earlier? Verify and watch the 'conspiracy' crumble.

options.text: If the puzzle fits this well, there must be a design behind it
options.explanation: Your brain is a pattern-finding machine - even where no patterns exist. You see faces in power sockets, rabbits in clouds, and conspiracies in random events. In a world of 8 billion people, 'incredible coincidences' happen every single day by pure statistics.

options.text: Coincidence doesn't mean causation. I look for direct evidence of coordination
options.explanation: Correlation does not imply causation. Two events happening at the same time aren't 'connected' until there's proof of intentional coordination. Ice cream sales and drownings both rise in summer - ice cream conspiracy?

options.text: The cinematic production makes me suspicious - who's funding this documentary?
options.explanation: A documentary with Hollywood-level production costs tens of thousands. Who's paying? An idealist? Or someone earning from views and monetisation? Following the money is always a good question.

questions.bonus_insight: Did you know? The human brain is wired to find patterns - an evolutionary advantage (better to flee a shadow than ignore a predator). But in the modern world, this 'superpower' becomes a vulnerability. Real conspiracies (Watergate, Panama Papers) are uncovered with documents, not with epic music and red lines on YouTube.

final_feedback.title: Your results
final_feedback.share_prompt: Share your result and challenge someone to beat your score.

0-8 points.title_card:
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
🛡️  BULLETPROOF
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Critical thinking: excellent
Blind spots detected: none
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0-8 points.description: Your critical thinking works the way it should. You recognise manipulation techniques, you don't let emotions drive you, and you verify before reacting. That's not common - most people have at least a few blind spots. Next step? Help those around you develop the same skills.
0-8 points.shareable_report:
🛡️ HOW MANIPULABLE ARE YOU?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Result: BULLETPROOF
Score: {{score}}/40
Propaganda resistance: excellent
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Take the test yourself →
suggestions: Your defences are solid - now keep them updated. Deepfakes and AI-generated content are the next frontier
Share these skills with those around you. Critical thinking can be taught

9-16 points.title_card:
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
🔍  HEALTHY SCEPTIC
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Critical thinking: good
Blind spots detected: a few
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
9-16 points.description: In most cases, your instincts guide you well. But some techniques - especially those working on emotions or perceived authority - still get through. That's not a flaw: these techniques are designed to fool everyone. Knowing where you're vulnerable is already half the defence.
9-16 points.shareable_report:
🔍 HOW MANIPULABLE ARE YOU?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Result: HEALTHY SCEPTIC
Score: {{score}}/40
Propaganda resistance: good
Weak spot: emotional traps
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Take the test yourself →
suggestions: Apply the 10-minute rule: before reacting to content that makes you angry, wait
Build a mental checklist: source? data? urgency? emotion? If there's no source, stop
Diversify your news sources - if you only read 'friendly' outlets, you're in a bubble

17-24 points.title_card:
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
🎯  EXPOSED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Critical thinking: needs strengthening
Blind spots detected: several
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
17-24 points.description: You're more vulnerable than you probably think. Not because you're 'gullible' - manipulation techniques are designed by professionals to work on everyone. The fact that you took this test means you want to improve, and that's already 50% of the work. Re-read the answer explanations: you'll find patterns that will help in real life.
17-24 points.shareable_report:
🎯 HOW MANIPULABLE ARE YOU?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Result: EXPOSED
Score: {{score}}/40

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Take the test yourself →
suggestions: Rule #1: If content makes you angry, don't share it. Ever. Verify first, always
Rule #2: If there's no source, it's not information - it's opinion
Install a browser fact-checking extension (NewsGuard, Media Bias/Fact Check)

25-40 points.title_card:
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Blind spots detected: significant
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
25-40 points.description: Your defences are low, but it's not because you're stupid. It's because manipulation techniques are designed by professionals and you don't yet have the tools to recognise them. Now you do. This test is the first step: re-read each answer's explanation and you'll be amazed how quickly you can improve.

suggestions: Golden rule: always verify before sharing. Always. Every single time
Take a free media literacy course on Coursera or edX
Use Full Fact and Snopes daily to train yourself to spot hoaxes
Mute WhatsApp group notifications to reduce urgency pressure

resources.name: NewsGuard
resources.description: Browser extension that rates news site reliability

resources.name: Media Bias/Fact Check
resources.description: Rates outlets on political bias and factual accuracy
